claimed_contribution: Demonstrates across three studies that consumers' self-regulation systems (promotion versus prevention) affect adoption of new and really new products.
Identifies boundary conditions: the regulatory-focus effect is strongest when new-product risk is not made salient and disappears for products portrayed as established.
Provides process evidence that performance-related uncertainty concerns mediate regulatory-focus effects on purchase intentions for new products.
context: The paper studies consumer goods adoption, including high-technology durables and newly launched repeat-purchase items. Experimental contexts include a really new car-boat (Aquada) and a digital camera framed as either new or established.
design_and_data: Study 1 is a mall-intercept field study with 250 consumers (ages 25-65) measuring chronic regulatory focus (RFQ) and self-reported ownership of new versus established products. Study 2 is a 2 x 2 lab experiment (n = 203 MBA students) manipulating regulatory focus (promotion/prevention) and risk salience (implicit/explicit) for a really new product, then measuring purchase intentions and coding open-ended thoughts. Study 3 is a 2 x 2 lab experiment (n = 166 undergraduates) manipulating regulatory focus and product newness (new/established camera ad), with Likert and percentage purchase-likelihood measures plus coded thought data. Both experiments test mediation with performance-uncertainty thought indices using ANCOVA and Sobel tests.
focal_constructs: new and really new product adoption
main_findings: Promotion-focused consumers reported higher ownership of many new high-tech and newly launched repeat-purchase products than prevention-focused consumers, while ownership of established products did not differ by focus.

For a really new product, purchase intentions were highest in the promotion-focus plus implicit-risk condition; when risk was explicitly highlighted, promotion- and prevention-focused participants showed similarly low purchase intentions.

Performance and use uncertainty concerns were more pronounced among prevention-focused consumers and mediated the regulatory-focus effect on purchase intentions for new products; for products portrayed as established, regulatory focus did not affect purchase intentions.
one_line_summary: Across one field study and two experiments, the paper shows that promotion-focused consumers are more likely than prevention-focused consumers to adopt new and really new products because they generate fewer performance-risk concerns unless risk is made salient.
relation_to_prior_work: extends prior adoption research that mainly emphasized aggregate innovation characteristics by introducing an individual-level motivational account based on regulatory focus. It also refines work on really new products by specifying when risk salience and product newness constrain regulatory-focus effects.
research_gap: Prior adoption research largely examined new-product purchase at an aggregate level, leaving individual-level psychological processes relatively underexplored. The paper addresses this by testing how self-regulation and perceived risk jointly shape adoption of new and really new products.
research_question: How do consumers' promotion versus prevention self-regulation systems influence adoption of new and really new products? The paper also asks whether this effect depends on whether product risk is made salient and whether it is limited to products perceived as new rather than established, and whether performance uncertainty explains the effect.
